coredata.dc:title: Blue fluorescent carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur as a dual-functional fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg2+ and chloramphenicol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hongxiang
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Mercury ions (Hg²⁺) contamination and chloramphenicol (CAP) residue in food pose significant threats to human health and food safety. This study explores the use of nitrogen and sulfur-doped blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) as a fluorescence-based sensor for the detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) and mercury ions (Hg²⁺), which are critical contaminants in both environmental and food safety contexts. B-CDs were synthesized and characterized, and their performance as sensors was evaluated through fluorescence intensity measurements in response to varying concentrations of CAP and Hg²⁺. The linear correlation coefficients (R²) for both CAP and Hg²⁺ detection were 0.9958 and 0.9944, respectively, demonstrating a strong linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and analyte concentration. The detection limits for CAP and Hg²⁺ were calculated as 0.31 μM/L and 1.75 μM/L, highlighting the high sensitivity of B-CDs. Additionally, the sensor's performance was tested under various conditions, including changes in temperature, salinity, and light exposure, showing excellent stability and photostability. This work provides a simple, efficient, and highly sensitive sensor for detecting trace amounts of CAP and Hg²⁺, with significant potential for applications in environmental monitoring and food safety. The findings may pave the way for the development of more advanced, cost-effective, and portable fluorescence-based sensors for broader analytical purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen and sulfur doping
Dual functional fluorescent probes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of B-CDs.

The structure characterization of B-CDs. (a) Photographs of B-CDs solution under natural light (left) and UV light (right); (b) TEM morphology; (c) XRD; (d) FT-IR spectrum; (e) XPS full spectrum; (f-i) High-resolution C1s, N1s, O1s, S2p spectra.

Optical characteristics of B-CDs. Excitation and emission spectra of (a) three-dimensional map and (b) two-dimensional projection; (c) Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum (purple), excitation spectrum (orange), emission spectrum (green). (d) Emission spectrum under different excitation wavelengths.

Optical Stability of B-CDs and the Effect of Solvents. Influences of (a) NaCl concentration; (b) temperature; (c) UV irradiation on the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs; (d) Fitting curve of integral fluorescence intensity against absorbance in different solvents; (e) Quantum yield in various solvents.

B-CDs as a Fluorescent Sensor for Detecting CAP and Hg2+ (a) Effects of various antibiotics and amino acids on B-CDs fluorescence intensity; (b) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs at different CAP concentrations; (c) Standard curve for CAP detection; (d) Effects of different metal ions on B-CDs fluorescence intensity; (e) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs at varying Hg2+ concentrations; (f) Standard curve for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Blue fluorescent carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur as a dual-functional fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg2+ and chloramphenicol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKey Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury ions (Hg²⁺) contamination and chloramphenicol (CAP) residue in food pose significant threats to human health and food safety. This study explores the use of nitrogen and sulfur-doped blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) as a fluorescence-based sensor for the detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) and mercury ions (Hg²⁺), which are critical contaminants in both environmental and food safety contexts. B-CDs were synthesized and characterized, and their performance as sensors was evaluated through fluorescence intensity measurements in response to varying concentrations of CAP and Hg²⁺. The linear correlation coefficients (R²) for both CAP and Hg²⁺ detection were 0.9958 and 0.9944, respectively, demonstrating a strong linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and analyte concentration. The detection limits for CAP and Hg²⁺ were calculated as 0.31 μM/L and 1.75 μM/L, highlighting the high sensitivity of B-CDs. Additionally, the sensor's performance was tested under various conditions, including changes in temperature, salinity, and light exposure, showing excellent stability and photostability. This work provides a simple, efficient, and highly sensitive sensor for detecting trace amounts of CAP and Hg²⁺, with significant potential for applications in environmental monitoring and food safety. The findings may pave the way for the development of more advanced, cost-effective, and portable fluorescence-based sensors for broader analytical purposes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, rapid industrial development has led to convenience but also environmental pollution from heavy metals, which poses great threats to human health and ecological safety. Mercury ions (Hg2+) are common heavy metal ions in nature, characterized by high toxicity and bioaccumulation potential. Elevated levels of Hg2+ in the human body can induce mercury poisoning, which can be life-threatening [1,2]. Thus, accurate monitoring of Hg2+ is crucial for controlling mercury pollution. In parallel, food safety has consistently been a major societal concern. Billions of people around the world face the risk of consuming unsafe food, with millions falling ill and hundreds of thousands dying each year. Moreover, foodborne diseases, often exacerbated by inadequate food safety measures, pose serious challenges to the health and financial systems of developing countries. As a result, food safety has become a global priority [3]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), contaminated food or water can lead to over 200 diseases, such as food poisoning, diarrhea, indigestion, and neurotoxic effects. Even low-level exposure can result in approximately two million deaths annually. Harmful substances that may remain in food include metal ions, pesticides, herbicides, veterinary drugs, illegal additives, and antibiotics. These contaminants can be introduced at any stage of food production, making convenient and rapid detection methods essential [4]. Chloramphenicol (CAP), a veterinary antibiotic, is prohibited in livestock farming and should not be present in food. However, some farmers and feed manufacturers still illegally use CAP, which can enter the soil through animal waste. Due to its stability, CAP may transfer to crops and vegetables, posing a potential risk to human health [5,6].

Fluorescence is a photoluminescence phenomenon [7], arising from the electronic structure and energy level distribution of materials. With its high selectivity and strong sensitivity, fluorescence offers unique advantages in analytical detection [8]. Fluorescence spectroscopy provides extensive information about materials, such as excitation spectra, emission spectra, three-dimensional spectra, and synchronous spectra. Over the past few decades, it has been recognized as one of the most sensitive techniques for trace analysis [9]. The high sensitivity, selectivity, and non-invasive nature of fluorescence technology make it an ideal tool for detecting and analyzing trace substances. As such, it holds significant potential for rapid, real-time, and non-destructive testing. Current fluorescence-based analytical methods, such as fluorescence immunoassays [10], fluorescence probe techniques [11], and fluorescence quantitative PCR [12], have found widespread applications in fields including medicine [13], food analysis [14], biochemistry [15], and environmental protection .

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), nanoscale carbon particles with diameters below 10 nm, exhibit remarkable fluorescence properties [16]. These particles are not only small and easily functionalized but also possess exceptional optical properties and biocompatibility[17]. Such characteristics make CDs a highly valuable research focus, especially in the past decade [18,19]. Traditional analytical techniques for environmental monitoring and food safety, such as chromatography (GC, LC) [20], tandem mass spectrometry (GC-MS and LC-MS/MS) [21], infrared spectroscopy [22], and Raman spectroscopy [23], provide precise and reliable results. However, they often require lengthy processing times, stringent sample preparation, expensive instrumentation, and skilled technical personnel. In contrast, fluorescence-based methods offer distinct advantages, including superior sensitivity, high selectivity, and non-invasive analysis, making them well-suited for detecting trace substances. As a result, optical probes based on UV–visible absorption or fluorescence emission are highly valued for their rapid and convenient detection capabilities. Compared to other inorganic fluorescent nanoparticles, CDs offer unique advantages, including an abundant and diverse range of precursors, simple synthesis procedures, stability, and versatile response mechanisms. In recent years, CDs have demonstrated vast application potential in areas such as environmental monitoring, food safety, and drug delivery, and more [24,25]. For instance, Kothalamzz et al. [26] developed a dual-sensor fluorescent sensor using cashew juice for detecting copper ions and the pesticide imidacloprid. Dong et al. [27] synthesized a two-photon carbon quantum dot nanoprobe via a hydrothermal method, which was used for quantitative detection of chloramphenicol in food. Manuel et al. [28] reported carbon dots capable of transporting the drug riluzole, enabling the observation of changes in biomacromolecules such as nucleic acids and protein conformations, laying a solid foundation for the development of targeted therapy for glioblastoma.

In this study, we synthesize nitrogen- and sulfur-doped blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) using a one-step solvothermal method with tartaric acid and 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid as precursors. The B-CDs exhibit a high quantum yield of 34 % and serve as a dual-sensor fluorescent probe for the detection of mercury ions and chloramphenicol. The sensitivity of B-CDs towards Hg²⁺ and CAP makes them suitable for environmental mercury ion monitoring and the detection of CAP in animal-derived foods, thus expanding their potential applications in environmental detection and food safety.

Tartaric acid, 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid, quinine sulfate, acetonitrile (ACN), ethanol (EtOH), isopropanol (i-PrOH), tetrahydrofuran (THF), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and ethyl acetate (EA) were purchased as analytical grade reagents from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Ethanol (EtOH) was obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. Metal ion samples (such as Pb2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, etc.) were sourced from the National Analysis Center for Nonferrous Metals and Electronic Materials. All aqueous solutions utilized in this study were prepared using double-distilled water produced in our laboratory.

The high-pressure reactor (25 mL) was obtained from Xiniou Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic cleaner was acquired from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd., while the electric constant temperature forced air drying oven was sourced from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis spectra were measured using a Shunyu Hengping 756 PC UV-visible spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Lengguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Using tartaric acid and 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid as precursors and water as the solvent, B-CDs were synthesized via a solvothermal method. Specifically, 0.1 g of tartaric acid and 0.2 g of 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid were added to 10 mL of water and ultrasonicated for 8 min to ensure thorough mixing. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a reaction vessel and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the product was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 25 min to remove impurities, followed by dialysis to obtain the purified B-CDs (Fig. 1 ).

Effect of Temperature on Luminescent Performance: B-CDs were added to water, and the fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs were recorded at temperatures ranging from room temperature to 70 °C, with measurements taken every 5 °C to capture the optimal fluorescence intensity.

Photostability Testing: A cuvette was loaded with 2 mL of B-CDs solution. The sample was irradiated continuously for 2 h with a UV lamp at 365 nm, positioned 5 cm from the cuvette. Fluorescence emission spectra were measured every 20 min during irradiation to record the maximum fluorescence intensity.

Salt Resistance Testing: At room temperature, NaCl solutions with concentrations of 0, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 mM were prepared. In a cuvette, 2 mL of each NaCl solution was sequentially added along with B-CDs, and fluorescence measurements were taken to determine the optimal fluorescence intensity.

Effect of Solvent on Luminescent Performance: In separate cuvettes, 2 mL of water (H₂O), acetonitrile (ACN), ethanol (EtOH), isopropanol (i-PrOH), tetrahydrofuran (THF), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and ethyl acetate (EA) were added. Then, 10 µL of B-CDs were transferred using a micropipette for fluorescence detection, and the optimal fluorescence intensity was recorded.

Using quinine sulfate (0.1 mol/L H₂SO₄ as solvent, fluorescence quantum yield QY = 0.54) as a standard[29], the absorbance (A) of the quinine sulfate solution and CDs was maintained below 0.05. The fluorescence quantum yield of B-CDs was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
φ U
=
φ S
·
F U
F S
·
A S
A U
·
(
η S
η U
)
2
=
φ S
·
k U
k S
·
(
η S
η U
)
2

Where φ represents the quantum yield of the CDs, quantifying their ability to convert absorbed light energy into fluorescence; F refers to the integrated fluorescence signal from the emission spectrum of the CDs measured in UV spectral analysis; A denotes the absorbance of the CDs at a specific excitation wavelength measured in UV-visible spectral analysis; k represents the slope obtained from the fitted curve of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance; η is the refractive index of the solvent used in the synthesis of the CDs, with subscripts S and U denoting the reference standard and the actual sample under test, respectively.

Milk and tap water samples were selected as real-world samples for analysis. The milk sample (Yili Full-Fat Sterilized Pure Milk) was purchased from the local supermarket. The tap water was collected from the laboratory at the Institute of Environmental Science of the university. A 10 mL aliquot of milk was treated with 20 mL of acetonitrile and sonicated for 15 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. The solution was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The resulting filtrate was diluted 10-fold with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) and stored for further analysis. The tap water sample was processed in the same manner as the milk sample. A 2 mL aliquot of the treated sample solution was mixed with 10 μL of B-CDs solution, followed by the addition of different concentrations of the analyte solution. The fluorescence intensity was then measured.

The B-CDs solution appeared nearly colorless under natural light but emitted a strong blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm), as shown in Fig. 2a . The morphology and chemical structure of B-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). TEM image (Fig. 2b) reveals that B-CDs are nearly spherical particles with an average diameter of approximately 8 nm and exhibit excellent dispersion. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2c) displays a broad and relatively low-intensity diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.9°, indicating that these carbon dots may possess an amorphous carbon structure. FT-IR characterization of the carbon dots is shown in Fig. 2d, where a strong absorption peak at 3839 cm−1 corresponds to OH stretching vibrations; the peak at 3741 cm−1 is attributed to NH stretching; the strong absorption peaks at 2345 cm−1 and 1735 cm−1 correspond to SH stretching and CO stretching, respectively. Absorption peaks at 1525 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are related to CC and CN stretching, while the peak at 595 cm−1 indicates CS out-of-plane bending vibrations, confirming the presence of thiol groups. XPS analysis of B-CDs showed four prominent peaks in the full scan spectrum (Fig. 2e) corresponding to O, N, C, and S, suggesting that B-CDs contain these four elements with relative atomic ratios of C/N/O/S = 41.24:6.47:28.89:4.47. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) reveals peaks for CC (284.7 eV), CN (285.8 eV), CO (287.2 eV), and CO3 2− (290.3 eV). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) exhibits peaks at 399.7 eV and 398.4 eV, attributed to NH and CN, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2h) displays characteristic peaks at 531.5 eV, 530.7 eV, and 530.1 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, and COH. The S2p spectrum (Fig. 2i) shows peaks at 167.4 eV and 168.6 eV, corresponding to CS and SO4 2−. FT-IR and XPS analyses indicate that B-CDs are multi-element doped carbon dots, possessing a variety of chemical functional groups and diverse chemical environments. Among these, nitrogen and sulfur doping are key factors influencing the photoluminescent properties of B-CDs. Nitrogen doping introduces sp²-hybridized nitrogen atoms, which can form strong π-π conjugation with carbon atoms, thereby enhancing the photoluminescence efficiency of the carbon dots. Sulfur doping further modulates the electronic structure and energy level distribution of the carbon dots, affecting both the emission wavelength and intensity. The presence of sulfur in B-CDs also facilitates the formation of additional defect states, which serve as effective luminescent centers, enhancing the fluorescence emission. Consequently, the surface of B-CDs is rich in functional groups such as NH, OH, CO, CC, and SH, which not only improve the hydrophilicity, stability, and luminescent potential of B-CDs, but also participate in the fluorescence emission process. Furthermore, the presence of carbonyl (CO) and thiol (SH) groups may provide active sites for specific interactions with target analytes, such as Hg²⁺ and CAP, which is crucial for achieving high selectivity in detection.

The optical properties of B-CDs were investigated using three-dimensional excitation-emission matrices (EEM), UV-visible absorption spectra, and fluorescence emission spectra. The EEM fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3a ) indicate the optimal emission and absorption spectra for B-CDs. From the EEM fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3b), the excitation wavelength range for B-CDs spans from 275 nm to 380 nm, demonstrating a broad excitation spectrum range that facilitates their application under various excitation conditions. The emission spectrum extends from 420 nm to 570 nm, covering the blue to yellow light regions of the visible spectrum. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3c) reveals a weak absorption band between 300 nm and 400 nm, attributed to the absorption of surface functional groups or defect states, with a shoulder peak at 300 nm arising from π-π transitions in the conjugated structure of the carbon core, which is one of the key mechanisms for carbon dot luminescence. A strong absorption band is observed between 200 nm and 300 nm, primarily due to σ-σ transitions of CC bonds, which is a characteristic absorption feature of carbon materials in the UV region. The optimal excitation wavelength for B-CDs was determined to be 345 nm, with the optimal emission wavelength at 471 nm. The overlap between the excitation and emission spectra is minimal, indicating little interference. As shown in Fig. 3d, the emission peak position of B-CDs remains unchanged with increasing excitation wavelength, suggesting that these carbon dots belong to a single emission center type, with their luminescent properties primarily determined by the conjugated structure within the carbon core and the surface functional groups.

The optical stability of B-CDs was evaluated by measuring their fluorescence intensity under various environmental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4a , the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots did not exhibit significant changes with variations in NaCl solution concentration. This stability is comparable to that reported for other carbon-based materials in the literature, such as the carbon dot-magnetic nanocomposite reported by Xie et al., which maintained stable fluorescence intensity even at high salt concentrations[30]. This demonstrates that B-CDs possess excellent salt tolerance, maintaining stable fluorescence properties in high-salinity environments. As shown in Fig. 4b, when water was used as a solvent and the temperature was gradually increased to 70 °C, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs exhibited minimal variation. In contrast, a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed for coumarin-based fluorescent probes under high temperatures, as reported by Wang et al. [31]. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs is therefore less sensitive to temperature changes, making them suitable for use in fluctuating environmental conditions. Additionally, the carbon dots were continuously exposed to UV light for 2 h, with fluorescence intensity measured every 10 min (Fig. 4c). The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs showed no significant change, unlike the nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots reported by Li et al., which exhibited a decline in fluorescence intensity after prolonged light exposure[32]. This indicates that B-CDs possess excellent photobleaching resistance, ensuring stable fluorescence performance during long-term storage and usage. This feature is particularly beneficial for applications in long-term monitoring and imaging.

In addition, we investigated the influence of solvent on the luminescent properties of B-CDs. The quantum yield, defined as the ratio of emitted photons to absorbed photons, is an important parameter for evaluating the fluorescence efficiency of a material. Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs at different concentrations were measured in several common laboratory solvents, including water, isopropanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, tetrahydrofuran (THF), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and ethyl acetate. The integral fluorescence intensity was plotted against the absorbance, as shown in Fig. 4d. Using a quinine sulfate (REF) solution as a standard (quantum yield = 0.54) [29], the quantum yield of B-CDs in different solvents was calculated, as shown in Fig. 4e. The quantum yield of B-CDs varied with the choice of solvent, with the highest quantum yield of 34 % observed in THF, and the lowest value of 7 % in DMF. This variation is likely attributed to an increase in non-radiative decay processes or interactions between B-CDs and solvent molecules. First, the increase in non-radiative decay processes may be a key factor influencing the change in quantum yield across different solvents. Non-radiative decay refers to the process by which excited species relax to the ground state without emitting radiation, such as through internal conversion or intersystem crossing, which competitively dissipate the excitation energy and reduce the quantum yield. In this study, we observed that B-CDs had a lower quantum yield in highly polar solvents, such as DMF, which could be due to enhanced charge transfer between the solvent and B-CDs or solvent cage effects that promote non-radiative decay processes[33]. Second, the interactions between B-CDs and solvent molecules may also play a role. In some solvents, B-CDs may form a solvation shell, which could alter their electronic structure and energy level distribution, thereby affecting their fluorescence efficiency . These findings highlight that the choice of solvent can significantly influence the fluorescence properties of B-CDs. Therefore, selecting appropriate solvent conditions is crucial for practical applications.

Given their excellent photoluminescent characteristics, rich surface functional groups, and outstanding chemical stability, B-CDs are promising candidates for sensing materials. To explore their potential, we investigated the selectivity of B-CDs and the effects of other metals, antibiotics, and amino acids on their fluorescence intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 5a , compared to other antibiotics, Chloramphenicol (CAP) significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs. Fig. 5b shows that as the concentration of CAP increases, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs decreases accordingly. The standard curve for CAP detection is presented in Fig. 5c, with the linear equation given by (F 0-F)/F 0 = 0.4557C CF (μM/L) + 0.0634 and a linear correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.9958. A good linear relationship was found between the normalized fluorescence intensity and CAP concentration within the range of 0.1–1.0 μM/L. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated using 3δ/k (where δ is the standard deviation of six blank samples and k is the slope of the linear equation) was determined to be 0.31 μM/L. CAP is a broad-spectrum antibiotic commonly used for the treatment and prevention of animal diseases. However, due to potential health risks associated with CAP residues in animals, strict detection of its levels in food is crucial. Therefore, we performed a spiking recovery experiment to evaluate the feasibility of using B-CDs for detecting CAP. Milk was chosen as the sample, and different concentrations of CAP were spiked into the samples, with each sample measured in triplicate. The results, presented in Table 1 , show recovery rates ranging from 80.0 % to 82.7 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 3 %, indicating good precision for the method.

Additionally, the addition of Hg2+ caused a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs (Fig. 5d). As shown in Fig. 5e, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs also declined with increasing concentrations of Hg2+. The standard curve for Hg2+ detection is illustrated in Fig. 5f, with the linear equation (F 0-F)/F 0 = 0.0434C(Hg2+ ) (μM/L) + 0.1004 and a correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.9944. A strong linear relationship was established between the normalized fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 1–8 μM/L, with an LOD of 1.75 μM/L. As industrialization progresses, environmental pollution, particularly from heavy metal ions and organic contaminants, poses a serious threat to ecosystems and human health. Due to the minimal impact of factors like salt concentration and temperature on the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs, they exhibit high stability and accuracy for practical environmental sample detection. This study demonstrated the feasibility of using B-CDs for detecting Hg2+ in water samples. Various concentrations of Hg2+ were spiked into the samples, which were measured in triplicate. The results, presented in Table 2 , indicate recovery rates between 99.4 % and 110.0 %, with an RSD below 5 %, showcasing the good precision and accuracy of the method, thus providing robust technical support for environmental pollution monitoring.

In this study, nitrogen- and sulfur-doped blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method and evaluated as dual-functional fluorescent probes for the detection of mercury ions (Hg²⁺) and chloramphenicol (CAP). The synthesized B-CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence, excellent stability, and a high quantum yield of 34 %. Structural characterization revealed their spherical morphology, multi-element doping, and diverse surface functional groups, which contributed to their remarkable optical properties and selectivity. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-CDs enabled sensitive detection of Hg²⁺ and CAP, with detection limits of 1.75 μM/L and 0.31 μM/L, respectively, and strong linear correlations in the tested concentration ranges. The B-CDs demonstrated outstanding photostability, salt tolerance, and temperature resilience, making them suitable for practical applications in environmental monitoring and food safety. Spiking recovery experiments further confirmed the precision and accuracy of this method in real-world samples, achieving recovery rates of 80.0 %–110.0 % with low relative standard deviations. Overall, the B-CDs presented in this work offer a simple, efficient, and cost-effective platform for detecting trace contaminants, showcasing great potential in developing portable and sustainable fluorescence-based sensors for broader analytical applications.
Hongxiang Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Shaogui Wu: Validation, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Keqing Zhao in College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A coumarin-based fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of hazardous mercury ions in living organisms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, Y. Ma, W. Lin, A coumarin-based fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of hazardous mercury ions in living organisms, J. Hazard. Mater. 461 (2024) 132604.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic use in livestock and residues in food—A public health threat: a review

Junaid, Antibiotic-resistant bacteria, antimicrobial resistance genes, and antibiotic residue in food from animal sources: one health food safety concern, Microorganisms 11(1) (2023) 161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Occurrence, toxicity and adsorptive removal of the chloramphenicol antibiotic in water: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.M. Nguyen, N.T.T. Nguyen, T.T.T. Nguyen, T.T. Nguyen, D.T.C. Nguyen, T.V. Tran, Occurrence, toxicity and adsorptive removal of the chloramphenicol antibiotic in water: a review, Environ. Chem. Lett. 20(3) (2022) 1929–1963.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removal of chloramphenicol antibiotics in natural and engineered water systems: review of reaction mechanisms and product toxicity

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An introduction to fluorescence correlation spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Resnick, An introduction to fluorescence correlation spectroscopy, Contemp. Phys. (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Superimposed” spectral characteristics of fluorophores arising from cross-conjugation hybridization
